cataloging_subject.label: Benedict Burgh's Cato Paraphrase
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the editorially joined Middle English Cato sequence printed by Max Förster and encoded as CME00106: the seven-stanza Parvus Cato, whose attribution to Benedict Burgh is disputed, followed by Burgh's Cato Major, a four-book rhyme-royal adaptation of the Latin Disticha Catonis. The two Middle English components have separate DIMEV records and distinct attribution evidence, so they are retained as parts of a composite cataloging unit rather than flattened into one securely authored work. The subject excludes the Latin source work, every physical witness and early print, Förster's eclectic edition and glossary, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Benedict Burgh's Cato Paraphrase
catalog_summary.author: Benedict Burgh; attribution disputed for Parvus Cato
catalog_summary.date: Cato Major probably composed or translated 1433–1440; Parvus Cato attribution and date unresolved
catalog_summary.region: England; no narrower work-level place asserted

assertion.notes: A normalized discovery title based on Förster's established German title. It does not erase the disputed attribution of the Parvus component.

scope.description: Förster's two-installment article and edition title, not a medieval title

titles.value: When I advertise in my remembrance / And see how fell

scope.description: DIMEV normalized incipit; witness spellings vary

titles.value: For why that God is inwardly the wit / Of man

scope.description: DIMEV normalized incipit; witness spellings vary

agents.name: Benedict Burgh
notes: Förster and modern catalogs identify Burgh as translator-adapter of Cato Major. Förster explicitly questions whether Burgh also made the appended Parvus Cato.
The role is work-level translation and adaptation, not scribal responsibility for any surviving witness.

assertion.notes: DIMEV catalogs the component under Burgh, but Förster says the attribution is uncertain and notes that the Parvus is absent from his reconstructed alpha branch.

scope.description: The 1905–1906 immediate source edition, not authorship of the medieval works

date_statements.display: Probably 1433–1440, composed for the young William Bourchier

assertion.notes: A work-level scholarly estimate, not a date copied from any one witness.

date_statements.display: Earliest securely cataloged witness window verified here: mid-fifteenth century, Durham Cosin MS V.ii.14

assertion.notes: This is the earliest broad production window among the currently cataloged witnesses directly verified in this research pass, not proof that no surviving witness is earlier.

date_statements.display: Earliest currently distinguished printed edition: Caxton STC 4851, circa 1476

assertion.notes: This is a print-publication statement, not a claim that the translation was composed for print. The relative order and dates of the three Caxton editions have changed since Förster.
Earlier manuscript and print witnesses transmit the work; this statement identifies the first complete critical edition verified here, not first textual publication.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The Latin Disticha is a related source work and often accompanies the English stanzas in witnesses. Förster's German study, Latin apparatus, and U-M's retained German glossary are edition layers, not primary languages of the cataloged Middle English composite.

form_statements.description: Predominantly seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), with an eight-line Parvus envoy and three eight-line ababbcbc Cato Major envoy stanzas.

subject_statements.label: Moral advice and conduct of life
Virtue and vice
notes: The work's practical moral topics include prudence, speech, friendship, household conduct, work, wealth, adversity, and reputation.

summaries.value: A Middle English verse adaptation of the moral schoolbook traditionally called the Disticha Catonis, presented as instruction from an elder to a younger reader on worship, family, learning, speech, friendship, anger, household management, work, money, adversity, reputation, and virtuous conduct. The sequence joins a short Parvus Cato of seven stanzas and an envoy to Benedict Burgh's much longer Cato Major, arranged in four books and ending with three envoy stanzas. Förster probably dates the major adaptation to 1433–1440 and connects it with the education of William Bourchier. The Parvus attribution remains uncertain. CME00106 encodes Förster's 1905–1906 eclectic text and German glossary, not a diplomatic transcript of any one medieval witness.

DIMEV separates Parvus Cato (6321 / NIMEV 3955) from Cato Major (1418 / NIMEV 854). Förster prints them consecutively but says the Parvus is absent from his alpha branch, is appended in Cambridge University Library MS Hh.4.12, and may not be by Burgh. The General Corpus record therefore uses one composite discovery unit with two explicit medieval work parts and does not treat the attribution as uniform.

The XML has 168 LG and 1,186 L elements. Its medieval verse extent is 168 stanzas and 1,185 verse lines: the extra L element carries the non-verse colophon 'Explicit liber parui Catonis' after stanza VII. The encoding begins at Förster's printed p. 303, omitting the article introduction on pp. 298–302; it continues through p. 323 of volume 115 and pp. 25–40 of volume 116, including Förster's editorial glossary on pp. 35–40.

extent.description: Three encoded content parts are represented for navigation: two medieval verse works and one editorial prose glossary. Parvus Cato has seven stanzas and 50 verse lines (six seven-line stanzas plus an eight-line envoy); Cato Major has 161 stanzas and 1,135 verse lines across four books. The repository XML records 168 LG and 1,186 L because the Parvus colophon is encoded as an additional L. The 82-paragraph German glossary counts as the one prose content part but is not a third medieval work.

content_parts.label: Parvus Cato
content_parts.summary: A short sequence of elementary moral precepts in six rhyme-royal stanzas and an eight-line envoy. Förster prints it from Cambridge University Library MS Hh.4.12 and explicitly questions Burgh's authorship.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[1]/DIV2[1], LG 1–7; verse milestones 1–50; the following colophon is a non-verse L

notes: DIMEV record 6321 describes this component as 1,185 lines, but that figure belongs to the full Parvus-plus-Major sequence and is internally inconsistent with the record's own short loci. It is not used as the Parvus extent.

content_parts.label: Cato Major
content_parts.summary: Burgh's four-book Middle English rhyme-royal adaptation of the Disticha Catonis, beginning at stanza VIII and ending at stanza CLXVIII with three eight-line envoy stanzas.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[1], from DIV2[1]/LG[8] through DIV2[4]/LG[50]; stanzas VIII–CLXVIII, verse lines 51–1185

content_parts.label: Förster's lexical glossary
content_parts.summary: German editorial notes on Middle English words and forms, retained by U-M after the verse but excluded from the medieval work extent.
notes: This part is represented for content navigation and edition boundary control. It is not counted as one of the two medieval work items.

related_works.title: Disticha Catonis
notes: The exact medieval Latin exemplar or exemplars used by Burgh are unknown.

Förster cites Emil Baehrens's 1881 Poetae Latini Minores III, pp. 214–235, for reference numbering while usually omitting the Latin from his English edition. That nineteenth-century edition is a scholarly comparison source, not Burgh's physical exemplar.
The anonymous Northern Middle English Cato in CME00083 is a distinct English version and is not merged into this composite.

evidence.citation: CME00106 XML source in the pinned CME submodule, structurally inspected 2026-07-13.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; BODY/DIV1[1] poem with four DIV2; BODY/DIV1[2] glossary; LG, L, P, PB, NOTE, MILESTONE
evidence.summary: Confirms identifiers, Middle English language declaration, exact encoded source boundary, four major divisions, 168 LG, 1,186 raw L, the Parvus colophon encoded as an extra L, 82 glossary paragraphs, page breaks, notes, and the 2016 encoding revision note.

evidence.citation: Max Förster, 'Die Burghsche Cato-Paraphrase,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 115 (1905): 298–323; 116 (1906): 25–40.
evidence.summary: Supports the composite boundary, Burgh attribution and date argument, disputed Parvus authorship, rhyme-royal form, exact edition and encoding extents, base witnesses, witness census, editorial method, stanza and line numbering, and glossary.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, records 6321 and 1418.
evidence.summary: Supports the two-record work boundary, identifiers, normalized incipits, subjects, verse forms and patterns, current witness census, witness loci, and early-print distinctions; the record-6321 1,185-line description is flagged as internally inconsistent.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, current catalog records for MSS. Rawl. C. 48, Rawl. poet. 32, and Rawl. poet. 35.
evidence.summary: Supports current shelfmarks, identifiers, broad dates, materials, Cato contents, and the partial-only status of public Rawlinson slide images.

evidence.citation: Durham University Library, current catalog and complete IIIF facsimile for Cosin MS V.ii.14.
evidence.summary: Supports current holding, mid-fifteenth-century date, complete codex description, Parvus and Major loci, Burgh attribution, 1433–1440 estimate, and textual omissions and variants.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Digital Library, complete IIIF facsimile of MS Ff.1.6 (Findern Manuscript).
evidence.summary: Supports current shelfmark, complete public facsimile, refoliated work locus, and direct visual confirmation that old ff. 181r–185v map to current ff. 155r–159v.

evidence.citation: SUB Göttingen, current catalog and complete IIIF facsimile for 8 Cod. Ms. philol. 163 n.
evidence.summary: Supports current shelfmark, fifteenth-century English origin, complete public facsimile, and visually corrected current work loci: Parvus ff. 1v–2r and Major ff. 2v–30r.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, current Peniarth MS 481 catalog and complete IIIF facsimile.
evidence.summary: Confirms that current Peniarth 481 was formerly Ancient Peniarth MS 38, resolving Förster's P shelfmark, and supplies material description, complete public facsimile, and visually audited Cato extent through f. 27r.

evidence.citation: ISTC and current bibliographic descriptions of the three Caxton Burgh Cato editions; Francis Jenkinson's 1906 facsimile of STC 4850.
evidence.summary: Supports distinction of STC 4851, 4850, and 4852, their approximate dates, the early print-attestation boundary, and the unique Cambridge copy underlying Jenkinson's facsimile.

evidence.citation: Emil Baehrens, ed., Poetae Latini Minores III (Leipzig: B. G. Teubner, 1881), 214–235.
evidence.summary: Supports the exact modern Latin reference edition and page span cited by Förster while preserving its comparison-only role.

open_questions.question: Was the Parvus Cato composed or translated by Benedict Burgh, or was it independently appended to the Cato Major tradition?
next_steps: Consult a modern critical study or edition that separately analyzes DIMEV 6321.
Compare the complete Parvus witnesses and their Latin framing without treating Förster's Hh.4.12 copy as an authorial original.

open_questions.question: What is the present identity and location of Förster's Raine manuscript Y?
next_steps: Trace the Raine manuscript's ownership after Förster and inspect York and National Library of Wales accession evidence.
Do not adopt DIMEV's tentative 'perhaps Peniarth 481' suggestion without resolving the conflict that current Peniarth 481 is explicitly former Ancient Peniarth MS 38, Förster's separate siglum P.

open_questions.question: What current stemma and critical text best supersede or refine Förster's 1905–1906 reconstruction across the enlarged DIMEV witness census?
next_steps: Consult Kuriyagawa's 1974 Caxton-based edition and later textual studies.
Keep DIMEV-only census additions collateral to Förster unless a source explicitly shows that he knew or used them.

open_questions.question: Can complete color facsimiles be obtained for Förster's two base witnesses, Cambridge University Library MS Hh.4.12 and Bodleian MS. Rawl. C. 48?
next_steps: Request whole-codex reproductions from Cambridge University Library and the Bodleian, including covers, boards, pastedowns, flyleaves, every folio, blanks, stubs, and edges where practical.
Record delivered-file terms separately from medieval-text copyright, map Rawl. C. 48 ff. 84–111v, and reconcile DIMEV's Hh.4.12 ff. 28v–30 with Förster's fols. 29v–31r within the complete objects.

notes: The work-level Middle English language and the German, Latin, and editorial layers of Förster's edition are deliberately separated.

Förster silently modernizes punctuation, regularizes capitalization, prints abbreviation expansions in italics, and emends Rawlinson C.48 extensively from a broad collation; CME00106 is therefore not a diplomatic witness transcript.
The four complete public manuscript facsimiles cached for this research are supporting witnesses, not proof that those codices directly supplied U-M's encoding. U-M encoded Förster's printed edition.

Peniarth 481's current catalog explicitly records the historical designation Ancient Peniarth MS 38. That evidence identifies it with Förster's P, which he had not seen, while leaving his separate Raine manuscript Y unresolved.
